source_claim: Madurai is approached through outer gardens, water bodies, fields and dawn ecology before the urban core
The city contains multiple sacred institutions and ritual spaces
Royal authority, temples and civic order define the waking capital
Moat, walls, guarded gates and military protection shape the urban boundary
Vaigai riverfront supports recreation, flowers, boats and seasonal life
Merchant institutions, luxury goods and specialised commercial streets operate within Madurai
Dance, music, performance traditions and trained artists form an urban cultural economy
Gold, textiles, grain, gems and craft products occupy specialised market streets
Outer settlement and righteous households provide protection for travellers
Mādhari offers Kaṇṇaki shelter through the pastoral hospitality tradition
Kōvalaṉ enters artisan streets and encounters the goldsmith quarter
The goldsmith falsely identifies Kōvalaṉ as the thief of the queen's anklet
A wrongful execution occurs without proper examination of evidence
Kannaki enters the royal court and proves the anklet was misidentified
The king recognises his failure of justice and collapses
Kannaki's grief becomes a public accusation against failed kingship
Kannaki invokes destruction of unjust Madurai while distinguishing protected innocents
The city undergoes divine and moral consequences after the broken royal order
